passages.explanation: Dorian's walk home crystallizes the novel's tragic irony. He longs for anonymity and the purity he sees in Hetty, yet his fame and beauty follow him everywhere. His admission that he told Hetty he was 'wicked' and she laughed reveals the impossibility of confession in his world: no one can believe that someone so beautiful could be corrupt. Wilde shows that Dorian's eternal youth has become a prison, not a gift, trapping him in a role he can no longer bear to play.

Dorian's prayer-like longing for his 'rose-white boyhood' and his recognition that the Faustian bargain was the source of all his failure represent the novel's moral thesis stated plainly. Wilde argues that consequence is not punishment but purification: 'there was purification in punishment.' By escaping the natural consequences of his actions through the portrait, Dorian lost the very mechanism by which human beings learn and grow. The wish that had seemed like liberation became the deepest form of imprisonment.

The destruction of Lord Henry's mirror is a powerful symbolic act. Dorian finally turns against the beauty he once worshipped, recognizing it as the instrument of his ruin. The mirror, a gift from Henry, represents the narcissism his mentor cultivated in him. But Wilde complicates the moment: is Dorian truly repentant, or is this self-loathing just another form of vanity? His reflection on youth as 'a green, an unripe time' inverts Lord Henry's lifelong worship of youth, but the destruction of the mirror cannot destroy what it reflected.

Dorian's mental accounting of the dead, James Vane, Alan Campbell, Basil Hallward, is chilling in its detachment. He rationalizes each death away: Vane is gone, Campbell chose his fate, Basil provoked him. Wilde exposes the self-serving logic of the guilty mind: every victim is reframed as either irrelevant or complicit. Dorian's claim that it is 'the living death of his own soul' that troubles him most sounds like spiritual awakening but may be just another form of self-absorption: even his guilt centers on himself.

The scene before the portrait is the novel's devastating climax of self-knowledge. Dorian hopes his one good deed will have begun to heal the painting, but instead finds new marks of cunning and hypocrisy. The portrait, Wilde's great metaphor for the soul's true record, cannot be fooled by performance. It reveals what Dorian himself finally admits: his renunciation of Hetty was vanity, his goodness was curiosity, his reform was just another mask. Wilde suggests that genuine moral change requires more than a single act; it requires the willingness to face the full truth of what one has become.

Dorian's decision to destroy the portrait rather than confess represents his final moral failure. He frames the destruction as liberation, killing the past to be free, but Wilde makes clear this is the ultimate act of evasion. The portrait has functioned as conscience itself, and to destroy it is to attempt to murder one's own soul. The knife that killed Basil now turns on Basil's work, completing a circle of violence. Wilde collapses creator, creation, and destroyer into a single fatal act.

The aftermath of Dorian's final act is witnessed from outside, through servants, passersby, and a policeman. Wilde pulls the camera back to the social world that never understood Dorian. The sneering gentlemen, one connected to Sir Henry Ashton, suggest that the gossip machine will process even this event into social currency. The public world will never grasp what happened in that locked room, just as it never grasped the truth behind Dorian's perfect face.

The final revelation is Wilde's masterstroke. The portrait is restored to its original beauty while Dorian's body reveals every sin, every year, every cruelty. The Faustian bargain reverses at the moment of death: the soul reclaims its truth and the body pays its debt. That the servants can only identify Dorian by his rings, the external markers of wealth and status, is Wilde's parting commentary on a society that only ever knew surfaces. The novel ends by insisting that the soul's reality is inescapable, no matter how long or cleverly it is hidden.
